turns.content: This feels a lot like my old job where we had to manage threads manually in C++, but Rust's async stuff seems more automatic. I'm looking at this tutorial trying to understand how FuturesUnordered actually works with streams.

You're right, it's a bit like that manual thread management, but instead of creating explicit threads, you use async tasks. The key difference is you don't create individual threads for each task; they run concurrently on the runtime's worker pool. For streams specifically, you can use buffer_unordered to control how many items are processed at once.
Okay, so I understand concurrency is automatic, but what exactly does FuturesUnordered do that a regular Vec<Future> doesn't? Is it just a smarter container?

Exactly. A regular Vec holds references to futures without executing them. FuturesUnordered actually spawns and collects the results of all those futures, handling completion events automatically. It's like having an executor built-in that tracks which tasks are done.

That sounds helpful but I'm still confused about how it interacts with streams specifically. The tutorial showed me using join_all on a stream, and then later trying try_join! but I couldn't get the syntax right.

join_all is used when you want to collect all results into a Vec immediately. It takes an iterator of futures and returns a Vec with all their values. For streams, try_join! is better because it allows the stream to continue even if some items fail.
Wait, so join_all consumes everything upfront? That means I can't handle errors gracefully in a streaming context?

You're absolutely right. If you use join_all on a stream, it will try to collect all results before the stream is fully consumed. This defeats the purpose of streaming since you lose the ability to handle partial failures or backpressure.
I see now that I made a mistake by thinking join_all works like an async version of map. But what about FuturesUnordered? Does it automatically buffer things?

FuturesUnordered doesn't auto-buffer in the sense of limiting concurrency; it just collects all completed futures. To actually control concurrency levels for streams, you need to use buffer_unordered explicitly.
Oh, so I can set a limit? Like max_concurrency = 10 or something?
Yes. You pass the concurrency level directly to buffer_unordered. This limits how many futures run simultaneously, preventing resource exhaustion.
But what if I want dynamic control? Like increasing concurrency when there's less work?
For that you'd need a custom executor or wrap your streams in a wrapper struct. The standard library doesn't expose direct dynamic adjustment of the buffer size.
I'm getting frustrated because I thought async was just like promises in JavaScript but simpler, but now I have to think about this buffering manually. Is there a way to make it feel more automatic?
You're right, it feels less automatic than some other languages because you need to explicitly manage concurrency levels. But the trade-off is better control over resource usage.
Can I use tokio::spawn with a thread pool instead? Or does that just create more threads?
tokio::spawn creates async tasks but doesn't inherently limit concurrency. You'd still need to manage that manually if you want bounded parallelism.
So the best approach is really just using buffer_unordered with a fixed number, right? And then I can't do dynamic scaling without writing my own logic?
Yes. For most use cases, you pick a reasonable concurrency level and stick with it. Dynamic scaling would require custom infrastructure beyond the standard library.
Okay so I should just go back to my tutorial and try buffer_unordered instead of trying join_all on streams again?
That's the right call. Remember, you want to avoid consuming all futures upfront which is what caused your confusion earlier.
Got it. One last thing - does buffer_unordered return a stream or just values?
It returns an async stream that yields results as they complete, maintaining the buffered concurrency you specified.

Imagine I'm processing a massive log file where each line triggers a network call. I want to run these calls concurrently but limit the total number of active connections at any time. What is the best way to handle this in Rust?

You can use FuturesUnordered with try_join! to manage a fixed pool of concurrent tasks, or you could leverage buffer_unordered on streams if your logs arrive continuously. The key difference is that FuturesUnordered relies on a known collection size upfront while stream buffering adapts dynamically based on the input rate.
I think I understand the static pool approach, but what about when my log file is huge and I don't know how many lines are in it beforehand? Should I just use join_all to batch everything?

join_all will collect all futures into a Vec and then execute them sequentially once the collection is complete, which defeats your concurrency goal. For unknown sizes, you should prefer buffer_unordered on streams because it processes items as they arrive without buffering everything in memory first.
But I need to ensure exactly N tasks run at once regardless of input rate. Can FuturesUnordered control concurrency level dynamically?

FuturesUnordered doesn't inherently limit concurrency; it simply collects futures and executes them based on the runtime's scheduling. To strictly cap active tasks, you need to wrap those futures in a channel or use tokio::spawn with a semaphore-like mechanism before spawning them.
That sounds complicated for my exercise. Is there a simpler pattern using buffer_unordered that I should try instead?

Yes, if you're processing a stream and want to throttle it, use the buffered stream adapter which internally manages a bounded channel between producer and consumer. This effectively limits concurrency without needing explicit semaphore code.
So for my book exercise, I should probably implement a simple version using FuturesUnordered with manual task count tracking rather than relying on buffer_unordered?

Exactly. For an intermediate exercise, manually track the number of spawned tasks and use tokio::sync::Semaphore to gate new spawns until slots are available. This teaches you explicit concurrency control better than relying on stream adapters.
